splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the timing rules for when actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability under ERISA must be finalized. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of updated information in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be fixed by the end of the prior plan year (the statutory measurement date), providing employers with greater predictability about potential liability.

circuits.name: Sixth Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts can apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises fundamental questions about judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, so that the period of absconding does not count toward the expiration of the supervised release term.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled
positions.summary: Holds that without explicit statutory authorization, courts cannot toll the supervised release term when a supervisee absconds, as doing so would effectively extend punishment beyond what Congress prescribed.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) from raising any claim they previously presented in successive collateral attacks, in addition to the separate requirements of § 2255(h). This affects whether federal prisoners must satisfy a stricter gatekeeping requirement than Congress may have intended.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the § 2244(b)(1) bar on previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, categorically barring them from re-raising any claim previously presented.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar does not apply to federal prisoners' § 2255 motions, so federal prisoners need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The split affects whether election administration rules can be challenged before elections take place or must wait until after potential harm materializes, with significant implications for mail-in voting and ballot counting deadlines nationwide.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to challenge election rules pre-election because the competitive injury from allegedly unlawful voting procedures is sufficiently concrete and imminent.

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates lack Article III standing to challenge ballot-counting rules because the alleged injury is too speculative and generalized before an election has occurred.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), or if possessing any one is disqualifying. This split leads to dramatically different sentencing outcomes for defendants with identical criminal histories depending on where they are prosecuted.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria, reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria is disqualifying, reading 'and' as distributive or disjunctive in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations in the criminal context, particularly after Kisor v. Wilkie.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the rule of lenity should prevail over deference to an agency's or the Sentencing Commission's interpretation of ambiguous provisions that carry criminal consequences. This split has deepened after Loper Bright overruled Chevron and implicates fundamental questions about the expansion of criminal liability through delegated interpretive power.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity prevails over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability without proper lawmaking.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference takes priority over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines or regulatory provisions with criminal consequences.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' commentary (Application Note 1) can expand the definition of 'controlled substance offense' under U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy, when the guideline text does not mention them.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to § 4B1.2, which includes conspiracy and attempt within 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation of the guideline entitled to deference under Stinson, or instead impermissibly expands the guideline's text. This affects career-offender designations for defendants with prior conspiracy convictions.

positions.label: Commentary validly includes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of § 4B1.2(b) entitled to deference, and conspiracy qualifies as a controlled substance offense for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary impermissibly expands guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition beyond what the text allows, particularly after Kisor narrowed deference to agency interpretations.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in law, such as the First Step Act's elimination of mandatory stacked sentences under 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), may be considered as an 'extraordinary and compelling reason' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law can contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar consideration of such changes, while others permit them alone or in combination with other factors. This split affects thousands of prisoners serving sentences that would be shorter under current law.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law may be considered, alone or in combination with other factors, as an extraordinary and compelling reason for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes categorically barred
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, whether offered alone or combined with other factors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state schedule includes a substance not controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires the underlying substance to be federally controlled, or whether any state-law drug offense qualifies regardless of whether the substance appears on federal schedules. This affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: No federal-law limitation required
positions.summary: Holds that 'controlled substance offense' under § 4B1.2(b) broadly includes any state-law drug offense, with no requirement that the substance be federally controlled.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that for a prior state conviction to qualify as a 'controlled substance offense,' the substance must be one that is controlled under federal law, applying a categorical approach.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that accrues between a Chapter 13 filing and conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the estate or the debtor. This split has significant practical consequences for debtors who convert in good faith, as it determines whether a trustee can sell appreciated assets to satisfy creditors.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the broad scope of 'property of the estate' under § 541(a), combined with § 348(f)(1)(A), means post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that under § 348(f)(1)(A)'s structure and legislative history, post-petition pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtor, not the estate, to protect good-faith converting debtors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a federal prisoner challenging conditions of confinement or BOP decisions affecting the terms (but not necessarily the fact or duration) of imprisonment must proceed under habeas corpus or may use a civil rights action (e.g., Bivens or the APA).
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists over whether challenges to conditions or terms of confinement — as opposed to the fact or duration of imprisonment — must be brought as habeas petitions or may be pursued through civil rights statutes. This affects federal prisoners' access to remedies when challenging BOP decisions such as denial of transfer to prerelease custody under the First Step Act.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to conditions of confinement that implicate the execution of a sentence must be brought under habeas corpus, not through civil rights statutes.

positions.label: Civil rights actions are also available
positions.summary: Holds that habeas is not the exclusive remedy for challenges to conditions of confinement, and prisoners may pursue civil rights claims or APA actions for BOP decisions that do not directly challenge the fact or duration of confinement.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may impose sentences upon revocation of supervised release that expressly punish defendants for the underlying violation of supervised release conditions, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to sanctioning the breach of trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the permissible purposes of sentences imposed upon revocation of supervised release. Some circuits allow courts expressly to punish for the underlying violation, while others limit the revocation sentence to addressing the breach of the court's trust. This affects hundreds of revocation proceedings annually and the severity of resulting sentences.

positions.label: Courts may punish for the violation itself
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly consider punishment for the underlying conduct that violated supervised release conditions, not just the breach of trust.

positions.label: Revocation sanctions limited to breach of trust
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences must be based on the defendant's breach of the court's trust in granting supervised release, not on punishment for the underlying violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal deadline for filing a notice of appeal applies to orders on Hyde Amendment motions for attorney's fees in criminal cases.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees — filed within a criminal case — is governed by the 30-day civil appeal deadline or the 14-day criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because the fee motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, but a minority of circuits apply the criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies (30 days)
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies (14 days)
positions.summary: Holds that because the Hyde Amendment motion arises in a criminal case, the shorter criminal appeal deadline governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that orders denying motions to seal medical records are immediately appealable as collateral orders because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' that cannot be vindicated after disclosure. Other circuits have taken different approaches to the collateral order doctrine's application in sealing contexts, creating potential for divergent treatment of privacy-related interlocutory appeals.

positions.label: Immediately appealable under collateral order doctrine
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal medical records is immediately appealable because medical privacy is a value of high order effectively unreviewable after the records are disclosed.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal is generally not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because post-judgment remedies may be adequate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas-type challenges — such as alleged legal errors in conviction or sentencing — can be considered as part of compassionate release motions, or whether such claims must be raised exclusively through § 2255 habeas proceedings. This affects prisoners who have exhausted habeas options but believe their sentences reflect legal errors.

positions.label: May consider habeas-type claims in compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider factors related to potential conviction or sentencing errors as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot use compassionate release for habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not serve as a vehicle for challenging alleged legal errors in a conviction or sentence, as those claims belong exclusively in habeas proceedings.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to' a broker's services, bars state-law negligence claims when a freight broker selects a negligent motor carrier. This has significant implications for personal injury plaintiffs and the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because they relate to the broker's services in arranging transportation.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they are generally applicable safety regulations, not regulations targeting broker services.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen, circuits have split on whether the longstanding prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances survives Second Amendment scrutiny when applied to marijuana users. Some circuits have upheld the statute while others have found it unconstitutional as applied.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) is consistent with the historical tradition of firearm regulation and does not violate the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons who use intoxicating substances.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label. This affects products liability litigation involving herbicides and pesticides nationwide, including high-profile Roundup litigation.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they parallel, rather than impose requirements 'in addition to or different from,' federal labeling requirements.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
